paragraphs.body: AI Should Not be Used to Create Music

Music has been recorded throughout history and in multiple different cultures. Even though these cultures had no way of interacting with or connecting with each other, music was present in almost every single one of them. Even when the cultures discovered each other but were unable to communicate due to a language difference, music was always enjoyed by both sides. According to Montagu, “from the Aurignacian period, which starts around 43,000 BP in eastern Europe and around 40,000 in the west, to the Magdalenian and later, ending around 10,000 BP, which we have a very considerable number of instruments, plus a few representations” (2017). Montagu explains that instruments and representations of those instruments have been found, which date back to 43,000 years before the year 1950. So, music is old, and with the advancement of the world so did music advance and improve. Today, with the advancement of AI we face the new rising problem of AI. AI (artificial intelligence) has advanced to a point where it can solve papers, generate images, and scour the internet for a specific type of information in seconds. Although all these advances are good, there is one that isn’t, and that is AI’s ability to compose music. People may not see it as a problem, but the use of AI in the composition of music is a problem because AI music doesn't affect listeners' emotions the same way human music does. Additionally, AI-generated music will raise challenges regarding ownership and copyright laws. A solution to these two problems is using musicians to create their own new and original music and then using AI to improve/perfect it.

annotations.comment: Please move the year of publication to right after the author's name. The page number is also needed.

paragraphs.body: The first point I’m going to talk about is AI’s ability to comprehend emotion compared to humans, and how humans’ way of using these emotions is better than AI’s. AI may be able to understand the concept of emotions. Fowler supports this claim by stating that “AI doesn’t possess the biological systems that generate emotions in humans. While it can mimic emotional responses, it doesn’t “feel” them” (2023). We know that most music is based on emotion, whether it’s happiness, sadness, or excitement, they all can be represented in music. This usually requires the music artist to actually feel the emotions and then express/turn them into the music that he is creating. While AI may be able to mimic these emotions, it will not be genuine, and this will affect how people will perceive the music, especially when it is easy to tell the difference between AI and humans. Especially when some people pay for their music either by premium subscriptions or by purchasing physical copies, people are going to be expecting something genuine and real. Artificial music produced by AI will also have a negative perception by professionals who can tell when something isn’t real, A study conducted by Moura states that “results from the online survey indicated a rather negative perception, low purchase intention for AI music and a negative credibility perception of musicians using AI. Findings from the experiment indicated no significant differences between the groups, suggesting that the awareness of the use of automation did not influence the perception towards the music.” (2021). The study even states that since there were no significant differences between the groups, people didn’t just choose the humans because they hate AI, but because the music made by AI just wasn’t that good compared to music made by humans. This also suggests that there is a creativity difference between humans and AI. AI might be smarter in the way that they have information from all over the web, but that doesn’t mean that they are creative, because they are programmed to think in a certain way. Humans, on the other hand, are different; every single human on earth is unique and has their own way of thinking, so music made by humans will be creative because it is based on how the artist thinks.

Montagu, J. (2017). How music and instruments began: A brief overview of the origin and entire development of music, from its earliest stages. Frontiers in Sociology, 2, 8. Fowler, G. A. (2025, April 2). Will AI ever feel emotions? The quest to understand machine consciousness. Medium. Tigre Moura, F., & Maw, C. (2021). Artificial intelligence became Beethoven: how do listeners and music professionals perceive artificially composed music?. Journal of Consumer Marketing, 38(2), 137-146. Latikka, R., Bergdahl, J., Savela, N., & Oksanen, A. (2023). AI as an artist? A two-wave survey study on attitudes toward using artificial intelligence in art. Poetics, 101, Drott, E. (2021). Copyright, compensation, and commons in the music AI industry. Creative Industries Journal, 14(2), 190-207.

annotations.comment: Please add a hanging indent of 0.5''

annotations.Peer Comment: 1. Purpose/Thesis: After reading the draft, what would you say is the purpose or thesis the paper is trying to get across? How does the draft fulfill (or not fulfill) that purpose? From Ali’s article, I clearly understand his statement about how AI can’t be a great music creator yet. He successfully finds sources supporting his claim and explains them very well. 2. Persuasiveness: What did you learn about the topic after reading the paper that you didn’t fully appreciate before you read it? Is this something you find significant or interesting? If so, why? How well did the author address counterarguments? After reading I understand why the market still value human musics more that Ai-made ones. I think Ali may spend more time on the logic chain from his reasoning to his conclusion. Like how Ali says “people didn’t just choose the humans because they hate AI, but because the music made by AI just wasn’t that good compared to music made by humans” 3. Support: Did you find the claims, reasons, and evidence in the document convincing? Why or why not? Did you see a balanced coverage/an even distribution of claims and evidence? What information can be omitted or added to help your peer focus on the thesis? (Include paragraph numbers). Ali’s body paragraphs have clear sturctures and I believe readers can easily distinguish claims, reasons, and evidence in the article. He did well on balancing evidences and the resons. He has every reasons followed by evidences. 4. Quoting and Paraphrasing: How effective has the author quoted and paraphrased information? Has the author used a signal phrase to introduce a quote/paraphrase and explain the quote/paraphrase in their own words? Quotations are introduced with signal phrases (e.g., “Fowler supports this claim...”), and explanations follow. However, some quotes—especially the longer one from Moura—could be paraphrased or shortened to improve clarity and maintain the writer’s voice. It would also help to discuss the significance of the quote more thoroughly afterward. 5. Organization: How is the draft organized in a logical manner (e.g., using headings/subheadings)? Given the organization, could the main points be presented in a more effective way? (Include paragraph numbers) The draft has a logical flow: introduction → emotional argument → perception study → conclusion. However, the structure could be improved by clearly separating the discussion of emotional authenticity from that of copyright and ownership, since the second major issue is barely mentioned after the intro. Adding subheadings like “Emotional Authenticity” and “Legal Challenges” might help the reader follow the argument more easily. 6. Transition: What suggestions can you make for transitions both between and within paragraphs that would make connections clearer and easier to follow? (Include paragraph numbers). Transitions between paragraphs are somewhat abrupt. For example, the transition from paragraph 1 to 2 could use a clearer signpost like, “One of the core challenges lies in the emotional disconnect between AI and human-created music...” Similarly, paragraph 3 jumps into survey results without a smooth bridge. Transitional phrases such as “Moreover,” “In contrast,” or “Another concern is…” can improve flow. 7. The sentences: Choose 1-2 sentences you consider the most interesting or best written— stylistically effective, entertaining, or otherwise memorable. Then choose 1-2 sentences you see as weak—confusing, awkward, or uninspiring. Advise your peer on how to revise the weak sentences. (Include paragraph numbers). Strong Sentences: “We know that most music is based on emotion, whether it’s happiness, sadness, or excitement…” (Paragraph 2): Clear and relatable. “Humans...are different; every single human on earth is unique and has their own way of thinking…” (Paragraph 3): A nice personalizing conclusion to the argument. Weak Sentences: “So, music is old, and with the advancement of the world so did music advance and improve.” Suggestion: “Music has evolved alongside human civilization, growing more sophisticated with each technological advancement.” “AI might be smarter in the way that they have information from all over the web…” Suggestion: “AI may access vast data online, but that doesn’t equate to genuine creativity.” 8. The format/design: To the best of your ability, check for and point out mechanical/grammatical errors, word choice issues, spelling mistakes, and APA formatting issues. Did the authors use formal academic language and a first/third person serious tone? (Include paragraph numbers). Use APA in-text citations consistently (e.g., Montagu, 2017 instead of just “Montagu”). The reference list needs to follow APA format strictly (check italics, capitalization, and date formats). Some grammatical issues appear, especially around subject-verb agreement (e.g., “music made by humans is” not “are”). The tone is mostly academic, but a few casual phrases (e.g., “that isn’t” or “just wasn’t that good”) could be revised for more formal tone. 9. Introduction/conclusion: What strategy/strategies have been used to introduce and conclude the draft? Does the introduction catch the reader’s interest? Does the draft conclude in a memorable way? How else might this draft begin and end? The opening is engaging with its historical overview of music and leads nicely into the problem of AI. Still, the thesis statement could be made more concise and explicit, e.g., “This paper argues that AI-generated music lacks emotional depth and raises copyright issues, and therefore should only be used to support human musicians rather than replace them.” Also, there isn’t a strong, standalone conclusion. A concluding paragraph that restates the main claims, reinforces the emotional and legal concerns, and reasserts the proposed solution would help end the paper more effectively. 10. Any other comments? You may add a stronger conclusion paragraph and expand on the “solution” mentioned in the thesis.
